coredata.dc:description: With the rapid changes in the field of information, the research and development of optical storage materials with high security and large storage capacity are particularly important in the development of contemporary society. However, conventional memory materials with static fluorescent outputs have the disadvantages of easy simulation and forgery, which limits their practical application in the protection of confidential information. In this research, a dual-stimuli-responsive intelligent fluorescent material based on Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions doped layered lanthanide hydroxide (LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA) was fabricated, which can realize reversible multi-color luminescence conversion (from green to red) by varying the pH and temperature. Combined with the Morse code and security pattern, the multiple encryption and decryption of confidential information and anti-counterfeiting can also be realized. Therefore, the obtained intelligent fluorescent material has a great application prospect for information security. In addition, due to the excellent color tunability, the material can provide the possibility to obtain potential fingerprints with high contrast and no background interference on different color substrates. The unique dual-stimuli-responsive behavior of this material provides more ingenious design inspiration for the design of multi-color intelligent fluorescent devices.

head.ce:title: A dual-stimuli-responsive intelligent layered lanthanide hydroxide for application in information security and latent fingerprint identification

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid changes in the field of information, the research and development of optical storage materials with high security and large storage capacity are particularly important in the development of contemporary society. However, conventional memory materials with static fluorescent outputs have the disadvantages of easy simulation and forgery, which limits their practical application in the protection of confidential information. In this research, a dual-stimuli-responsive intelligent fluorescent material based on Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions doped layered lanthanide hydroxide (LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA) was fabricated, which can realize reversible multi-color luminescence conversion (from green to red) by varying the pH and temperature. Combined with the Morse code and security pattern, the multiple encryption and decryption of confidential information and anti-counterfeiting can also be realized. Therefore, the obtained intelligent fluorescent material has a great application prospect for information security. In addition, due to the excellent color tunability, the material can provide the possibility to obtain potential fingerprints with high contrast and no background interference on different color substrates. The unique dual-stimuli-responsive behavior of this material provides more ingenious design inspiration for the design of multi-color intelligent fluorescent devices.

Combined with Morse code and security mode, Tb3+ and Eu3+ ion-doped layered lanthanide hydroxide (LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA) can realize multiple encryption and decryption and anti-counterfeiting of confidential information. In addition, due to their excellent color tunability, the material can provide the possibility to obtain potential fingerprints with high contrast and no background interference.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para:
The rapid development of big data shows us a modern society with the rapid development of information technology. At the same time, the consideration of data storage capacity and security performance has increasingly become the focus of attention. 1–6 At present, some information storage technologies, such as radio frequency identification technology, 7 nano-humidity sensing technology 8 and laser holography technology 9
,
10 , have some shortcomings, such as complex operation, inconvenient use and high cost, which greatly limit the application in information security storage. Recently, photofluorescent materials based on organic dyes, 11 carbon dots (CDs), 12
,
13 semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), 14
,
15 and rare earth nanoparticles, 16–19 with their well photoluminescence properties and low cost have been widely reported for information security and anti-counterfeiting. However, these photoluminescence materials with static fluorescent outputs are usually visible in ambient light, near infrared light or ultraviolet light, 20–22 which is conducive to simulation and forgery, limiting their practical application in the protection of confidential information.

Stimuli-responsive intelligent fluorescent materials can produce changes in fluorescence spectra or fluorescence lifetime according to specific external stimuli (including light, temperature, magnetism, electricity, and pH, etc.), 23–26 which can be an ideal candidate material to prevent tampering or forgery. However, for some stimuli-responsive materials, the change of fluorescence signal is irreversible, so as the changed fluorescence can no longer be restored. 27 During data storage, the encoded information will be irrevocably destroyed rather than temporarily hidden. Therefore, the development of stimulus-responsive intelligent fluorescent materials capable of reversible fluorescence signal conversion is of great significance in advanced encryption and information storage. Lanthanide ions are considered to be ideal emitting sources as the outstanding optical activities, such as line-like emissions and large Stokes shifts. 28 However, the emission intensity of lanthanide ions is weak. When the chelate is formed with the organic ligand, the energy will be transferred to the lanthanide ions, and the emission intensity will be significantly enhanced. 29 Because the kinds of lanthanide ions such as Tb3+ (green light emission) and Eu3+ (red light emission) ions, and organic ligands are adjustable, by introducing stimuli-responsive elementary ligands into the process of material assembly, a variety of responsive lanthanide complexes with different fluorescent colors can be obtained under external stimulation. However, pure lanthanide complexes have some shortcomings such as poor photostability and thermal stability, which limits the promotion in anti-counterfeiting. Fortunately, the introduction of rare earth ions into the inorganic matrix and the chelation of ligands to assemble the lanthanide-based materials are expected to overcome these defects. 30–32 Layered lanthanide hydroxides (LLHs) are one kind of lanthanide-doped fluorescent nanomaterials combined the positive rare earth based hydroxide layers and negative corresponding anions. 33
,
34 LLHs combine the special properties of lanthanide ions and guest intercalated anions, and have been widely applied in medicine, 35 catalysis, 36 and sensors 37 due to their unique electronic structure and excellent thermal stability. The use of a variety of external stimuli to reversibly modulate the fluorescence properties of these lanthanide-based material, is expected to increase the capacity and security of information storage.

Herein, we developed a dual-stimuli-responsive intelligent fluorescent material based on a Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions doped layered lanthanide hydroxide (LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA) (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, the Tb3+ and Eu3+ lanthanide ions are stabilized within the network of layered yttrium hydroxide. Then, the intercalation of ligand (dipicolinic acid, DPA) by the anion exchange reaction enhances the optical properties of LLHs. This intelligent material shows different fluorescent colors in the presence of external stimulation (pH and temperature). The application of this intelligent material realizes dynamic high-density anti-counterfeiting under both pH and temperature stimulation, which significantly improves the complexity and security of information encryption. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of this material are reversibly modulated under extrinsic stimuli, which enhances the capacity and security of information storage. Furthermore, this fluorescent nanomaterial can be used to detect latent fingerprints on different substrates with high visual resolution. The unique dual-stimuli-responsive behavior of this material provides a new idea and theoretical basis for the development of multi-color intelligent fluorescent materials.

All rare earth nitrates were analytically pure and purchased from Shanghai Energy Chemical. Dipicolinic acid (DPA, 99.0%) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium nitrates (NaNO3, 99.0%) were acquired from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Co., Ltd. Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, ≥99.0%) was purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd. Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.8%) was purchased from Tianjin Weichen Chemical Reagent Science and Trade Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were collected on a Tecnai-G2-F30 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) were recorded with a Rigaku-Dmax 2400 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation over the 2θ range of 5°–60°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the materials were carried out within the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range by using a Bruker V70 FTIR spectrometer with the standard KBr disk technique. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed by a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer. Contents of rare earth elements were analyzed by an inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission (ICP-AES) spectrometer (Ultima2 HORIBA Jobin Yvon). CHN elemental analyses were measured on an Elementar Vario EL analyzer. The luminescence spectra in phosphate buffers with different pH values were collected on a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer at the 280 nm excitation wavelength.

The synthesis method of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x materials was optimized according to the previous literature. 38 In a typical reaction, Y(NO3)3·6H2O (1 mmol), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (1 mmol, the molar ratio of Eu (III)/Tb (III) = 0:1, 1:5, 1:1, 5:1, 1:0, respectively), NaNO3 (26 mmol), and HMTA (2 mmol) were dissolved in 23 mL deionized water. The above aqueous solution was stirred at 90 °C for 12 h and the obtained precipitate was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol three times and dried in vacuum.

DPA-intercalated LYH:Eu x Tb1–x (LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA) was prepared by the exchange reaction between nitrate and DPA anions. The LYH:Eu x Tb1–x product (120 mg) and DPA (0.34 mmol) were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water, followed by the addition of sodium bicarbonate (17 mg) to increase the solubility of DPA in water. After stirring at 90 °C for 12 h, the solid products were recovered by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol three times and dried in vacuum.

A total amount of 200 μL LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA solution (2 mg/mL) was added to 1800 μL of phosphate buffer with pH of 3–10, respectively. In order to study the reversibility of the response of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA to pH, LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA was dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (pH = 3) of 100 mL, and the pH of the solution was modulated by adding different volumes of H3PO4 or NaOH. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded under 280 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent inks, encryption and decryption of information and anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of fluorescent inks for writing brush was as follows. Firstly, 600 mg polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was added to 10 mL distilled water and fully dissolved by ultrasound for 2 h. The obtained uniform solution was divided into two parts and the pH was adjusted to 3 and 10, respectively. Finally, 200 mg LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA wet products were added to the above two solutions respectively, and the green and red fluorescent inks were obtained by ultrasonication for 1 h. In the preparation of fluorescence coding, the above fluorescent inks with pH = 10 and the red inks sold in the market were encoded together to achieve secure and rapid encryption and decryption of related information. The encrypted information was decrypted and re-encrypted in the presence of hydrochloric acid (HCl) and triethylamine (TEA) vapor. In the anti-counterfeiting process, a series of anti-counterfeiting patterns, such as carrot, apple tree and Tai Chi, were printed on the quantitative filter paper with less background interference by using pens and stamps with a suitable combination of fluorescent inks with pH = 3 and green inks sold in the market. The part of the anti-counterfeiting patterns displayed a reversible color change in the presence of HCl and TEA vapor.

The total amount of 200 μL LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA solution (2 mg/mL) was added to 1800 μL phosphate buffer (pH = 4.0), the temperature of the solution was changed and the fluorescence spectra varying regularly from 20 to 80 °C were recorded (λ ex=280 nm). In order to ensure the accuracy of the data, the interval of each test was 10 min to ensure that the test system was maintained at the set temperature.

The volunteers were asked to gently rub the parts of the face that are prone to secrete grease, such as the forehead and nose, with the belly of their fingers, and then press the fingertips on different substrate surfaces with appropriate pressure, including stainless steel plate, post-it notes, toughened film for mobile phone, mobile phone charger, USB flash disk, rubber gloves, visiting card, notebook cover and so on. To develop the potential fingerprint, dried LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA powder was applied to the area where the fingerprint was printed, and then gently brushed off the excess powder along the ridges of the fingerprint with a banister brush. Finally, the developed fingerprint patterns were obtained by 254 nm ultraviolet light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure properties of layered lanthanide hydroxides, LYH:EuxTb1–xDPA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the TEM, HRTEM, PXRD patterns and FTIR spectra of corresponding samples. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the TEM image of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA indicates that the sample is composed of stacked nanosheets with a maximum lateral dimension of approximately 100–300 nm. The HRTEM image clearly shows the lattice fringes of LLH, and the interplanar spacing of 0.308 nm is attributed to the (220) plane of LLH (Fig. 1(b)). 39 The PXRD patterns of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA samples are displayed in Fig. 1(c). A series of sharp and symmetrical (00l) reflections and non-(00l) reflections are the characteristics of the layered structure and are in good agreement with the patterns reported in the previous literature. 40 In the intercalation of DPA anions into the LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 gallery, a prospective shift to lower diffraction angles and the spacing increases from 0.898 to 1.35 nm, which certifies the successful replacement of NO3 – by DPA anions.

As displayed in Fig. 1(d), the stretching vibration peaks of OH from interlayer water and hydroxide layers at 3579 and 3387 cm−1 are located in FTIR spectra of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5. Meanwhile, the absorption peak of the bending of water molecules appeared at around 1634 cm−1. The pointed peak around 1384 cm−1 is assignable to the ν 3 mode of the nitrate species. 41 The vibrational band at 3387 cm−1 is red-shifted to 3437 cm−1, indicating that the hydrogen bond is formed between DPA and hydroxide layers. 42
,
43 Moreover, the strong band of nitrate vibration (1384 cm−1) is correspondingly weakened when DPA anions are intercalated into the layered host (Fig. 1(d)), which proves the interaction between host LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and DPA anion. This can also be confirmed by the UV–vis absorbance spectra of DPA, LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA (Fig. S1). The absorbance spectra of DPA have a peak shifts and intensity changes when DPA is exchanged to the LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 interlayer. It is generally believed that the change of the absorbance spectrum indicates that the organic ligand should have been coordinated with the metal ion. 42
,
44 After exchanging with DPA anion, the retention band at 1384 cm−1 indicates the coexistence of DPA anion and NO3 –. The asymmetric and symmetrical stretching vibration peaks of COO– group are observed at 1627, 1573, 1446, and 1384 cm−1, indicating that the DPA anions are intercalated into the interlayer. 42 The molar ratio of Y:Eu:Tb in the obtained material is 1:0.46:0.47, which is consistent with the theoretical value. The chemical composition of the corresponding intercalated DPA can be also determined by the contents of C, H and N (Table S1). It is found that the content of N element is greatly reduced and the content of C element is correspondingly increased after exchanging with DPA, indicating that the DPA molecules replace the nitrate anions in the interlayer. 42

According to the XPS characterization of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA, the composition and chemical bonds were investigated. The full spectra (Fig. S2) of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA show that the peaks at 156.9, 407.5, 531.6, 1134.2 eV and 1165.6, 1241.9 and 1276.7 eV are assigned to Y 3d, N 1s, O 1s, Eu 3d and Tb 3d, respectively. The detailed C 1s and N 1s spectra of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5 and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA are displayed in Fig. 2 . After DPA intercalation, C 1s can be divided into four peaks, which are CC bond at 284.4 eV, C–C bond at 285.3 eV, C–O/C–N at 287.5 eV and CN/CO at 288.4 eV. 45
,
46 Compared with LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5, the high resolution spectra of N 1s of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA show C–NC at the energy band of 398.2 eV, 47 which further indicates that DPA anions are successfully intercalated into the interlayer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the LYH:EuxTb1–xDPA (x = 0, 0.165, 0.5, 0.835, 1) layered material
ce:section.ce:para:
The excitation and emission spectra of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA in phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7) and powder sample are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and Fig. S3, respectively. The layered material contains two kinds of ions, Eu3+ and Tb3+, and the corresponding optical behaviors of these two ions can be observed in the emission spectra. The emission peaks observed at 579, 592, 615, 649 and 693 nm correspond to the 5D0→7F J transitions of Eu3+, while the bands of 489, 543, 582, 620 and 646 nm belong to the 5D4→7F J transitions of Tb3+. 48 Among the above emission peaks, the emission peak of Tb3+ at 545 nm and the emission peak of Eu3+ at 616 nm correspond to green emission and red emission, respectively. It is well known that the triplet energy level (TEL) of DPA ( 3 ππ∗, 25510 cm−1) is higher than that of Eu3+ (17300 cm−1) and Tb3+ (20500 cm−1). 49
,
50 According to Latva's rule, the best energy level difference from ligand to rare earth ion, that is, Δ E
=
π
3
π ∗
–
D J
5
is 2500–5000 cm−1. Therefore, the energy level difference between the TEL of DPA and Eu3+ is too large, resulting in insufficient energy transfer to Eu3+. It is observed in LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA that the strong emission of Eu3+ is due to the energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a), the DPA ligand can absorb ultraviolet light and produce a π-π transition, that is, the electrons first transit from the singlet ground state (S0) to the singlet excited state (S1), and then to the triplet excited state (T1) in the form of non-radiative transition. 51 Subsequently, the energy is transferred from the ligand to the lowest excited state (5D4) of Tb3+, and the electrons in the lowest excited state of Eu3+ may come from the T1 of the ligands and the 5D4 of Tb3+. Eventually, the excited electrons return to the ground state of the rare earth ions, leading to red emission and green emission, respectively. 52 This process is further verified by the lifetime decay curves of Eu3+ emission under a 280 nm excitation.

As shown in Fig. 3(b), both lifetime decay curves of LYH:Eu0.5DPA and LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA are fitted through the following double-exponential equation 38 :

The average decay times of Eu3+ ions in samples are obtained in Fig. 3 (b) by the above Eq. (2). In the presence of terbium ion, the average decay time of Eu3+ ions increases a lot, demonstrating that there is obvious energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+ in LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA. 42

tIn order to determine the optical response of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA to pH, the fluorescence spectra of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA (x = 0, 0.165, 0.5, 0.835, 1) in phosphate buffer solution of different pH were recorded. With the change of pH from 3 to 10, there is an obvious difference in the emission of Eu3+ and Tb3+ under the excitation of 280 nm. In particular, the characteristic emission of Tb at 545 nm decreases with the increase of pH, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu at 615 nm increases significantly (Fig. S4(a, b)). The emission spectra of the response of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA to phosphate buffer solution with different pH are shown in Fig. 3(c). Compared with LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA (x = 0.165, 0.835) (Fig. S4(c, d)), LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA displays better fluorescence properties for the change of pH. The Commission Internationale de l’éclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram in Fig. 3(d) clearly shows the fluorescence color change of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA, and the corresponding color coordinate values are also displayed in Table S2. When the pH increased, the fluorescent colors shift from the green region to the red region. The digital luminescence photographs of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA solution under 254 nm ultraviolet lamp are consistent with the results of chromaticity diagram. The fluorescence colors of the LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA solution present a regular change from green to red as the pH values increase from 3 to 10 (Fig. 3(e)). According to previous studies, 52
,
53 the pH response of the luminescence displayed by the samples are due to the change of the triplet excited energy level of DPA. The lowest triplet energy level of DPA is more suitable for energy transfer from the ligand to central metal ions to sensitize the fluorescence of Tb3+ ions. Under alkaline conditions, the deprotonation of DPA leads to the decrease of the triplet excited energy level, which is only slightly higher than the corresponding 5D4 level in Tb3+, and matches well with Eu3+. Therefore, the effective transfer of energy to Eu3+ occurs under alkaline conditions, resulting in the change of fluorescence color of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA. Furthermore, the pH reversibility of the LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA layered material in the range of pH 3 to 10 was also studied. The pH was initially adjusted to 3, and the ratios of fluorescence intensity at 616 and 545 nm were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 3(f), the increase of pH value to 10 leads to the increase of fluorescence intensity ratio, which could be quickly restored as the pH value is reduced to 3. The sensing process could be reversibly carried out for at least 5 cycles, indicating that LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA is a highly reversible pH sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic circuit of the normalized fluorescence intensity for pH sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
On the basis of the above research, LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA can be constructed as a logic gate to analyze the change of pH value. A one-to-two decoder, which can convert one input signal into two output signals, was designed based on the fluorescence response of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA to pH value (Fig. 4 (a)). As an advanced analytical device, putting two output signals into four logic groups: NOT (0, 1), PASS 0 (0, 0), PASS 1 (1, 1) and YES (1, 0) can reflect the change of pH value. As shown in Fig. 4(b), eight pH values of 3–10 are set as input signals, and normalized luminescence intensities of 545 and 616 nm (which represented the emission of green and red) are selected as double output signals, and the pH = 5.5 (Fig. 4(c)) is the threshold. The PASS 0 gate is triggered by pH = 5, the YES gate is triggered by pH = 6 and the NOT gate is carried out when the pH value is less than 5. Therefore, the output NOT (0, 1) indicates low pH, the output YES (1, 0) indicates a high pH value, and any other code PASS 0 (0, 0) or PASS 1 (1, 1) reflect the medium pH of 5–6. In addition, the emission colors derived from the color coordinate of the digital photos can clearly show different pH values, such as green (pH = 3), yellow-green (pH = 3–5), orange-yellow (pH = 5–7), orange-red (pH = 7–9) and red (pH = 10) (Fig. 3(e)). The constructed logic circuit has a broad application prospect in computer-aided environmental pH monitoring. 27
,
54
,
55

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of fluorescent encryption, decryption and re-encryption as well as anti-counterfeiting in information dissemination
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA, we combined it with the rules of Morse code to extend its potential application to the field of information security. 56
,
57 First, the red fluorescent inks with pH = 10 and the stable commercial fluorescent inks (not easy to change the fluorescent color due to external stimulation) were employed as encryption and stable basement to ensure that the information will not be easily disclosed. During the first encryption, the Morse code for the word “CHEMISTRY” was written in red fluorescent inks, however, a string of misleading messages “RKSAUZBLYW” was generated due to the presence of commercial inks. Since these two inks have the same green fluorescence under the 254 nm UV lamp, the encrypted information cannot be identified (Fig. 5 (a)). In the decryption process, the fluorescent color of the encrypted word “CHEMISTRY” Morse code is converted to green after exposure to HCl vapor and could be clearly read. Because the LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA mentioned earlier has good pH reversibility, the key information “CHEMISTRY” could be re-encrypted into a string of error messages by exposure to TEA vapor, which shows good security in information dissemination, and the relevant personnel can read and erase the information in time. As shown in Fig. S5, the decryption-re-encryption process is very rapid, and the fluorescent color change can be realized in 30 s, and become the most obvious within 3 min.

Taking advantage of the pH reversibility of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA, it is also suitable for anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the anti-counterfeiting pattern of carrot drawn with a pen consisted of two parts, the green fluorescent inks with pH = 3 as carrot, and the commercial green inks as leaves. The original pattern shows green fluorescence under the excitation of 254 nm. Interestingly, it is clearly observed that the carrot part turned to red and the leaf part remained unchanged after treatment with TEA vapor, and the carrot part is retransformed into green when exposed to HCl vapor. As shown in Fig. 5(c, d), the stamp-printed anti-counterfeiting patterns are also coded with green fluorescent ink with pH = 3 and green commercial ink, which appears green color under a 254 nm UV lamp. The three apples of the apple tree and half of Tai Chi pattern can also become red after exposure to TEA vapor, and they return to green again after exposure to HCl vapor. These interesting luminescence phenomena indicate that LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA can adjust the luminescence of anti-counterfeiting labels through acid-base stimulus response, thereby increasing the difficulty of being forged. The above results demonstrate that compared with the reported work, 58 the prepared layered materials can obviously improve the confidentiality in the process of message passing and anti-counterfeiting security.

To investigate the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA layered materials, we measured the fluorescence emission spectra of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA at different temperatures. Fig. 6 (a) indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak of Tb (Ⅲ) at 545 nm and the characteristic peak of Eu (Ⅲ) at 616 nm both decrease with the increase of temperature in the range of 20–80 °C, and the decrease of Tb (Ⅲ) is more obvious than that of Eu (Ⅲ). Due to the different temperature responses of Eu and Tb, LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA may have excellent ratio temperature sensitivity. In the heating process, the value of I 616/I 545 shows a rising trend. The experimental data of I 616/I 545 are shown in Fig. 6(b), the red solid line is the fitting curve, which follows the empirical function as:
(3)

/
I 545
=
A
T 3

+ D

in which A = 7.64 × 10−6, B = −8.37 × 10−4, C = 0.029, D = 0.99 and the correlation coefficient R
2 = 0.998. However, the value of I 616/I 545 is reduced (Fig. 6(c, d)) during the cooling process, and the regression equation is

(R 2 = 0.996). In addition, during the heating process, the digital photos of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA under 254 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. S6) show that the fluorescence color of the solution changed from yellow-green to red and its brightness decreased gradually with the increase of temperature, which is consistent with the results of the above fluorescence spectra. Based on previous reports, 59
,
60 the good temperature-sensitive fluorescence properties may be derived from photoexcited electron relaxation. The obtained results also indicate that LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA could be used as an excellent fluorescent thermometer.

Due to the small size and the excellent luminescent property of the as-prepared LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA nanocomposite, the layered nanomaterial was applied to latent fingerprint detection. Because the chemical components of fingerprints include hydrophilic and hydrophobic substrates such as inorganic salts, amino acids, and lipids, 61 the as-prepared LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA powder is quickly adhered onto the latent fingerprint and a clear and complete fingerprint is developed by scanning the excess powder along the fingerprint lines with a soft brush (Fig. 7 (a)). This powder development method for detecting the latent finger print is facile and rapid due to no complex operations and expensive instruments involved in the whole process. In fingerprint detection, fingerprints are donated by volunteers who gently rub fingertips on their foreheads and noses with minimal pressure, tamping them on different substrates. Because of their particularity and immutability, fingerprints are usually vital evidences to confirm criminal suspects in criminal investigation. 62 The particularity of fingerprints usually includes the following three levels: level 1 shows a rough picture of the flow of the ridges in the fingerprint, which is not unique but can play a role in screening and exclusion. In level 2, more details in the fingerprint can be observed, including termination, bifurcation, core, etc., which can be used for individual identification. Level 3 displays all the features of the fingerprint, such as sweat pores, which are also personalized. 63

The main evaluation indexes for accurate and rapid extraction of potential fingerprints are as follows: imaging contrast, sensitivity, individual specificity and overall versatility. Because stainless steel is usually the material for making murder weapon in forensic investigation, we chose stainless steel plate, a representative smooth substrate, for fingerprint imaging to confirm the high contrast and selectivity of the prepared LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA layered material. First, the gray value at the line section can represent the contrast between the ridges and furrows of the fingerprint (Fig. 7(b)). As shown in Fig. 7(c), the gray values of ridges and valleys vary greatly, indicating that LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA is conducive to enhancing latent fingerprint imaging. 64 As shown in Fig. 8 (a, b), the clear fluorescence ridge flows and approximate outlines (level 1) can be observed in the image of potential fingerprint under the excitation of 254 nm, and there is no background interference, which indicates that LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA layered material has high selectivity. It is worth mentioning that the multi-color fluorescence emission of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA can be realized by changing the ratio of Eu3+/Tb3+. Meanwhile, the excellent color tunability of the above materials makes it possible to obtain potential fingerprints with excellent contrast and overall versatility in different environments. Previously reported traditional fluorescent materials are difficult to show high contrast in complex and changeable environment, 65
,
66 so the as-prepared LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA with high contrast and selectivity may have the potential to become a novel fluorescent material in screening potential crime scenes and capturing fingerprint clues.

In order to confirm the high sensitivity of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA layered materials, we also amplified the image in Fig. 8(b) and studied the details of the latent fingerprint in three levels. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the outline and ridge trend of the fingerprint can be clearly observed (level 1). As shown in Fig. 8(d), the level 2 characteristics at different locations of the fingerprint can be illustrated clearly at a higher magnification including four different details. More importantly, the friction ridge of the fingerprint clearly shows the sweat pores in the third-level characteristics of the fingerprint, and the determination of the specific characteristics of the individual provides an important guarantee for the confirmation of individual identity. It is quite difficult to determine their details according to other reported methods, especially level 3. 67 It should be noted that all the detailed fingerprint information in the whole process are extracted without complex and tedious post-processing operations, which would improve the efficiency of latent fingerprint imaging. Such a time-saving visualization may be of practical help to forensic investigations.

To demonstrate the wide applicability of the LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA layered material for latent fingerprints detection, the imaging performance of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA for latent fingerprints applied on various substrates was investigated, including colorful post-it notes, notebook cover, agate mortar, visiting card, rubber gloves, USB flash disk, mobile phone charger, toughened film for mobile phone. The potential fingerprints were developed on five different color paper-based substrates to explore the influence of different color background on fingerprint visualization. As shown in Fig. 9 , all fingerprint patterns can clearly show fingerprint ridges and approximate contours. Subsequently, the development of invisible fingerprints on different substrate materials were further studied. Fig. 10 shows the optical fingerprint image obtained by applying a series of multi-color fluorescent materials to these different colors and material substrates. All developed fingerprint patterns show bright fluorescence, and the clear fingerprint ridges and outlines with excellent contrast and versatility could be distinguished. The clear fluorescence imaging of fingerprints developing on different color substrates can be attributed to the tunable color and higher fluorescence intensity of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA, which is of great help to expand the application of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA in practical environment.

In addition, fingerprints left at the crime scene are sometimes not discovered in time, and the stability of fingerprint visualization is also a key indicator. 68 Based on this requirement, LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA is applied in two different situations, one is to place the developed fingerprint for a week, the other is to age the fingerprint for a week, and then develop the fingerprint with LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA. Compared with fresh fingerprint (Fig. 11 (a)), clear fingerprint ridges and rough outline are observed in both cases (Fig. 11(b, c)), indicating that it has high stability and broad practical application prospects.

In summary, dual-stimuli-responsive intelligent fluorescent material based on a lanthanide ions doped layered lanthanide hydroxide was synthesized by homogeneous precipitation method and applied to the field of multiple information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. According to the good pH response of LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA layered materials, a one-to-two logic gate circuit with pH value as input and two fluorescence emissions as output signals was constructed, which was used to detect the change of pH value in the environment by changing the emission color from green to red. The fluorescent color of the ink made from LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA layered materials can be adjusted through the repeated stimulation of acid-base gas. Combined with the Morse code and security pattern, the multiple encryption and decryption of confidential information and anti-counterfeiting can also be realized. Moreover, LYH:Eu0.5Tb0.5DPA has excellent ratio temperature sensitivity in the range of 20–80 °C, which shows great potential in temperature sensing. In addition, this multi-color fluorescent materials with excellent color tunability are applied to latent fingerprint development, and the fingerprint development patterns with high contrast, high selectivity and wide applicability can show clear details of level 2 and level 3. Based on the excellent and unique optical properties of LYH:Eu x Tb1–x DPA, it is expected to become a multi-functional fluorescent material, which may has a broad application prospect in pH sensing, temperature sensing, information security, anti-counterfeiting and fingerprint identification.
